items.question: Is logistic regression regression or classification?
items.answer: Classification. It models P(y=1|x) with sigmoid output and is trained with BCE. The name regression refers to linearity in log-odds space: log(ŷ/(1−ŷ)) = wᵀx + b.

items.question: Write the sigmoid function and the binary cross-entropy loss for one example.
items.answer: Sigmoid: σ(z) = 1/(1+e^(−z)) with z = wᵀx + b and ŷ = σ(z). BCE: −y log ŷ − (1−y) log(1−ŷ) for y ∈ {0,1}.

items.question: Why is minimizing BCE equivalent to maximum likelihood for binary labels?
items.answer: Bernoulli likelihood P(y|ŷ) = ŷ^y (1−ŷ)^(1−y). Negative log-likelihood per example is exactly BCE. Averaging and minimizing BCE maximizes the product of Bernoulli likelihoods over the dataset.

items.question: What is the decision boundary of logistic regression?
items.answer: The hyperplane where wᵀx + b = 0, equivalently ŷ = 0.5. It is linear in features — logistic regression cannot model nonlinear boundaries without feature transforms.

items.question: When would you report AUC instead of accuracy for a logistic regression model?
items.answer: When classes are imbalanced, when false positives and false negatives have different costs, or when you need threshold-free ranking of ŷ scores — AUC measures separability across all thresholds (see ROC & AUC topic).
